title: FunKit: A computer algebra toolkit for functional approaches
abstract: We introduce FunKit, a Mathematica package for the derivation and tracing of functional equations from arbitrary master equations. FunKit provides an expression vocabulary and a set of rules that allow for derivations in any given field theory and master equation. It also allows users to add extensions for more specific equation systems. Therefore, it can be used in a wide range of situations, for example Dyson--Schwinger or functional RG equations, flowing reparametrisations, nPI equations, (modified) STIs and WTIs, functional Polchinski and Wegner flows, functional master equations with sources, and many others. Besides interfacing with the \FORM language to trace large tensor expressions efficiently, FunKit also provides facilities to export arbitrary Mathematica expressions to C++, Julia or Fortran code, including the results of derivations, which can then be evaluated numerically. Both the tracing and code generation can also be used independently and in combination with other packages.
summary: This paper introduces FunKit, a sophisticated Mathematica toolkit designed to automate the derivation and tracing of functional equations in Quantum Field Theory. It allows physicists to systematically calculate complex quantities like flow equations and Dyson-Schwinger equations for various models. By integrating symbolic manipulation with code generation (C++/Julia/Fortran), FunKit provides a powerful, end-to-end computational platform for advanced theoretical physics research.
main_problem: The paper addresses the complex task of deriving and systematically tracing functional equations arising from arbitrary master equations in Quantum Field Theories (QFTs). It aims to provide a comprehensive computational framework for these derivations.
main_result: The authors introduce FunKit, a powerful Mathematica package that automates the entire pipeline, from theory specification and functional differentiation to tensor tracing and code generation for numerical evaluation.
method: The methodology involves defining the theory's field content and master equations, followed by sequential computational steps: taking functional derivatives, applying systematic truncations, routing momenta, and finally tracing the resulting large tensor expressions.
model_or_system: The toolkit is applicable to a wide range of QFT functional approaches, including Dyson-Schwinger equations (DSEs), functional Renormalization Group (fRG) equations (like the Wetterich equation), $n$PI equations, and Slavnov-Taylor identities. Examples include Yang-Mills and scalar theories.
key_observables: Correlation functions ($\Gamma_N$), propagators ($G_{ab}$), flow equations ($	ext{Flow2F}$, $	ext{Flow4F}$), and the 1PI generating functional ($\Gamma$).
important_parameters: The calculation depends on the chosen regulator functions ($R_{ab}$), coupling constants ($\lambda[i]$), and the specific truncation scheme applied to the infinite set of diagrams.
main_assumptions: The process relies on defining a global setup, specifying truncation rules, and assuming the ability to systematically manage large diagrammatic expressions through computer algebra.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the overall FunKit workflow, showing the pipeline from theory specification through functional derivation to final numerical code generation.
paper_structure: The paper details the toolkit's functionality across several sections, covering notation (Section 2), basic syntax (Section 3), tensor bases/diagrams (Section 4), code generation (Section 5), and culminating in full examples demonstrating the calculation of complex flow equations (Section 6).
why_interesting: While focused on QFT, the systematic, automated handling of complex functional derivatives, tensor contractions, and code generation is highly relevant to any field requiring the symbolic manipulation of high-dimensional correlation functions or master equations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: FunKit workflow: From theory specification to numerical evaluation.

Figure 2: Results of the Yang–Mills example in examples/Yang-Mills/. Left: Inverse gluon dressing 1/ZA(p) compared to lattice data from [43]. Center: Ghost dressing in the scaling limit. Right: Avatars of the strong coupling.

Figure 3: Performance comparison between FunKit (v1.0.0), DoFun (v3.0) [27, 28] and QMeS (v1.2) [29]. In all cases, we measured the total time to take the derivatives and truncate the expression. Especially for very large expressions with several thousand diagrams, e.g. the six-point and four-point flows, FunKit excels. All benchmarks were performed on a 16-core AMD Ryzen 9 7945HX machine. Two warmup-cycles were used before five benchmark runs, which led to the error bars as shown in the plots.
